titleStmt.title: The Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive

respStmt.orgName: University of South Carolina Provost Humanities Grants
University of South Carolina Department of English
Center for Digital Humanities at the University of South Carolina
Magellan Scholar Program at the University of South Carolina
National Society of the Daughters of the American Revolution
Carnegie-Whitney Grant from the American Library Association

availability.p: Periodicals and books published before 1 January 1923 are in the public domain. If re-publishing, quoting from, or reproducing images from this digital archive, we request that you cite it as follows: Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive, Center for Digital Humanities, University of South Carolina.

Periodicals and books published on or after 1 January 1923 may be protected by U.S. Copyright Law. All attempts to secure permission for republishing post-1923 material in this archive have been made. The names, logos, and identifying images, as well as the content of the articles, remain the property of the periodicals and/or of the authors and artists themselves. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder. Archival material published in this archive is used with permission of the institution or library that holds it. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder.
Responsibility for any use of the contents of this digital archive rests exclusively with the user.

msIdentifier.repository: William Dane Papers, Houghton Library, Harvard University
msIdentifier.idno: Logbook of the "Alert," Manuscript AmW 84, volume 4

availability.p: By permission of the Houghton Library, Harvard University.

div.p: Continuation of the weather of yesterday. Crew variously Employed in ship[']s duty. The Boys trying out the [sea] Elephant blubber on shore. At the landing place of St Pedro there is but one house, which is occupied by a Mr Foster and family and is kept for the purpose of receiving and depositing goods for shipment and for the accommodation of passengers, the nearest village being the town of the angels above mentioned which is over 30 miles dist there are a number of Rancho's [sic] situated between, the nearest of which is about 4 miles from the beach.

At Foster's there is an old Indian, and as he is the last of his race, [he] is an object of interest[.] he was one of a tribe that formerly inhabited the Islands which form one side of the canal of Santa Barbara, all of which give evidence of having once been populated[.] In 1825 the Island of St Nicholas was the only one that had not become depopulated, (how they became so is not known) at this time there were on St Nicholas about 30 men and 23 women. during that year a party of Russians and Kodiacks from the Russian settlements on the NW of about 25 men were left at these islands to hunt Otter, and after having many quarrels with the Indians of St. Nicholas respecting the women, the Russians at length killed all the men with the exception of this old fellow who was badly wounded, having his head split open and a number of charges of buck shot fired into him. He however managed to escape, the Russians then became possessed of the women and lived with them about a year, when having an opportunity to gratify the revenge, which may sleep in an Indian but never dies, they destroyed every one of the Russians and Kodiacks in their sleep. About 4 or 5 years since there was but three women and (Black Hawk as my Boys have named him) the Indian above mentioned left and Capt Robbins (from whom I have the account) calling there with his vessel persuaded two of the females to go [to] St Pedro. the [sic] other ran away to the mountains and it is said is sometimes seen by the hunters who still visit the island, but is too wild to be approached. Black Hawk seems to be noncompass and the wounds on his head are the probable cause.

While trying out the blubber to day on the beach the old fellow smelt out what was going on (he is also blind) and after eating at least 5 lbs of the raw stinking material to begin with, he continued to gorge himself with the scraps as they were tried out, till he could contain no more, when he stretched himself out on the rocks with a hot sun shining in his face and after chattering and laughing in his idiotic manner for three hours - he again resumed his station at the Try-pot, where he made a sumptous dinner - and before dark, he was enabled to partake of a supper - which wasin proportion to his Dinner & breakfast. The blubber yielded about 75 Gals of oil.
